icd_category: C64.9 — Malignant neoplasm of kidney, unspecified (nephroblastoma)
one_liner: A 3-year-old boy is brought in by his mother after she felt a firm lump on the left side of his abdomen while bathing him. He is otherwise well and playing normally.

stage2.hpi_extension: Mom noticed the mass two days ago. He has not complained of pain. He is eating and voiding normally, with no hematuria reported. He has had low-grade subjective fevers and seems slightly more tired over the past two weeks, but remains active. No vomiting, diarrhea, or weight loss. No recent trauma. Pregnancy and delivery were uncomplicated, and he has met all developmental milestones.
stage2.pmh: Born at term. Noted at birth to have a small **hemihypertrophy of the right leg** and a remote history of neonatal hypoglycemia.
stage2.family_history: No childhood cancers. Paternal grandfather with lung cancer.
stage2.social_history: Lives with parents and older sister, attends daycare. Up to date on vaccines.
stage2.physical_exam: General: well-appearing, interactive toddler. HEENT: normal, **no aniridia**. Cardiovascular: regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Lungs: clear. **Abdomen: large, smooth, firm, non-tender mass palpable in the left flank that does not cross the midline**; no peritoneal signs. Genitourinary: normal external genitalia, testes descended. **Right lower extremity slightly larger than left (hemihypertrophy)**. No lymphadenopathy.

lab_results.result: **Abdominal ultrasound**: large (10 cm) heterogeneous solid mass arising from the left kidney, displacing normal renal parenchyma. **CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast**: **9.8 cm heterogeneously enhancing intrarenal mass** arising from the left kidney with areas of necrosis; tumor appears confined to the kidney with an intact pseudocapsule; no renal vein or IVC thrombus; no lymphadenopathy. **Chest CT**: no pulmonary metastases.
lab_results.clinical_significance: A large, intrarenal, heterogeneous solid mass in a young child (2-5 years) is **Wilms tumor (nephroblastoma) until proven otherwise**. Differential includes **neuroblastoma** (suprarenal, crosses midline, encases vessels, calcifications) — Wilms displaces rather than crosses the midline.

lab_results.result: Specific gravity 1.018, pH 6.0. Blood trace. Protein negative. Glucose negative. **No catecholamine metabolites elevated** (VMA/HVA normal).
lab_results.clinical_significance: **Normal urinary VMA/HVA argues strongly against neuroblastoma**, which secretes catecholamines in ~90% of cases. Minimal hematuria is common in Wilms.

lab_results.result: WBC 9.4, hemoglobin 10.8 (mild anemia), platelets 380. Peripheral smear normal. No blasts. Coagulation studies normal (no von Willebrand-like deficiency).
lab_results.clinical_significance: Mild anemia is common; a baseline CBC is important before surgery and chemotherapy. Wilms can rarely be associated with acquired von Willebrand disease.

stage5.final_clue: He undergoes **left radical nephrectomy**. Surgical pathology: **Wilms tumor (nephroblastoma)** with **classic 'triphasic' histology consisting of blastemal, stromal, and epithelial components**, confined to the kidney (favorable histology, stage I). Genetic testing identifies a **WT1 mutation on chromosome 11p13**. He receives adjuvant vincristine and actinomycin-D per protocol with an expected cure rate >90%.

debrief.classic_presentation: **Wilms tumor (nephroblastoma) is the most common primary renal malignancy of childhood** and classically presents in children aged **2-5 years** with a **painless, smooth, firm, unilateral abdominal flank mass** — typically discovered by a parent while bathing or dressing the child. Associated findings include **abdominal pain, hematuria, hypertension** (from renin secretion), and occasional fever. Associated syndromes include **WAGR syndrome (Wilms tumor, Aniridia, Genitourinary anomalies, and Range of developmental delays/intellectual disability)** — caused by deletion of **WT1 on chromosome 11p13**; **Denys-Drash syndrome** (Wilms + diffuse mesangial sclerosis + pseudohermaphroditism, also WT1); and **Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome** (macroglossia, organomegaly, **hemihypertrophy**, omphalocele, neonatal hypoglycemia, from imprinting defects at 11p15 involving **WT2**).
debrief.pathophysiology: Wilms tumor arises from **embryonic metanephric (nephrogenic) blasts** that fail to differentiate into mature renal tissue. **Classic 'triphasic' histology** consists of **blastemal** (undifferentiated small round blue cells), **stromal**, and **epithelial (primitive tubules/glomeruli)** components. The key tumor suppressor genes are **WT1 (11p13)** — mutated in WAGR and Denys-Drash — and **WT2 (11p15)**, implicated in Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome. Wilms tumor characteristically **displaces rather than encases** adjacent structures on imaging, helping to distinguish it from neuroblastoma. It can spread hematogenously to the lungs.
debrief.treatment_overview: Treatment is multimodal and highly effective. **Radical nephrectomy** is the cornerstone, with biopsy avoided preoperatively to prevent tumor spillage (which upstages disease). **Adjuvant chemotherapy** (vincristine and actinomycin-D, with doxorubicin added for higher-stage disease) and **radiation therapy** are added based on stage and histology (favorable vs. anaplastic). **Overall cure rate approaches 90%** for favorable-histology disease. Children with predisposing syndromes undergo surveillance abdominal ultrasound.
high_yield_pearls: Wilms tumor = most common **primary renal malignancy in children**, peak age **2-5 years**; classically a **painless, smooth flank mass that does not cross midline**.
**Triphasic histology**: **blastemal + stromal + epithelial** components derived from **metanephric mesenchyme**.
**WAGR = Wilms + Aniridia + GU anomalies + Retardation**; from **WT1** deletion on **11p13**. **Denys-Drash** = Wilms + gonadal dysgenesis + mesangial sclerosis (WT1).
**Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome**: macroglossia, organomegaly, **hemihypertrophy**, omphalocele, neonatal hypoglycemia → increased Wilms risk (**WT2 on 11p15**).
Contrast with **neuroblastoma** (younger kids, **crosses midline**, encases vessels, elevated urinary **VMA/HVA**, N-myc amplification). Wilms has excellent prognosis (~90% cure).
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Hematology and Oncology chapter, 'Childhood tumors — Wilms tumor (nephroblastoma)' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Renal chapter, 'Renal tumors in children' (WAGR, Denys-Drash, Beckwith-Wiedemann associations).
